business_info: Older print runs of a card may share the same name and rules text but are still considered distinct products for merchandising and inventory purposes; however, unless otherwise specified, internal analytics treat all printings of the same `card_name` as a single logical card when aggregating performance metrics.

For historical reasons, the `power` column sometimes stores values like "*" or "1+*" for modal or scaling cards; these values are only meaningful for gameplay rules explanations and should not be used in numeric aggregations unless a separate rules engine is involved.

Cards that appear as both a promo and a regular edition in the same release window are counted once for legality checks but may have duplicated rows in the catalog tables; analysts usually de-duplicate by `oracle_id` or a similar rules-level identifier when they care about unique gameplay pieces rather than physical SKUs.

In internal reporting, a card is described as "evergreen" if it has had at least one printing per three-year block, but this label is not stored directly in the database and has to be inferred from release history if needed.

Marketing sometimes refers to certain highly sought-after cards as "tentpoles"; this is a purely descriptive term based on perceived player interest and has no specific mapping to any database column or flag.

Some internal dashboards use a calculated "metagame share" percentage to rank cards, but this metric comes from external tournament data feeds and is never persisted in the core catalog schema; it must be recomputed or joined from the analytics layer when needed.

The legalities table may record niche or deprecated formats created for specific events (e.g., "Block 2019" or special in-store leagues); these event formats should not be interpreted as globally supported play formats for standard analytics unless explicitly required.

A small subset of cards were printed only as part of third-party collaboration products and may have unusual legality flags; these cross-branded cards should generally be excluded from comparisons where the business question assumes mainline product only.

Internal documentation sometimes uses the phrase "modern-plus" to describe formats that allow cards from approximately the same era as the modern format but with relaxed ban lists; this phrase is not associated with any single legality flag and cannot be derived from the schema alone.

Product managers may refer to certain experimental formats as "beta formats" during testing phases; if those formats ever go live they receive official names that may or may not be stored alongside the legacy internal labels in the database.

When a format is sunsetted, its legality flags are usually left in place for archival purposes rather than being deleted; analysts should be careful not to treat these archived formats as actively supported unless they are specifically scoping for historical play environments.
The release date used in the catalog is the first sanctioned release date for play in any region, which may predate general retail availability in some markets by one or more weeks.

Some anniversary or celebration products are given a symbolic release date of January 1st for that year if they were sold continuously or via multiple waves throughout the year; this convention is only for reporting convenience and does not reflect a precise street date.

Cards distributed exclusively as part of early access or convention promos may be assigned a placeholder release date tied to the parent set; there is no separate column that marks these as early access beyond the product or set naming conventions.

The catalog contains both double-faced and split cards; for these, the `name` column may contain a combined value like "Card A // Card B", and internal tools sometimes treat the front face as the primary identifier for search and ranking.
For cards with multiple faces, business reports typically attribute power and toughness values to the front face only, unless the analysis explicitly targets back-face-only gameplay scenarios.

Inventory and catalog systems sometimes use a concept called a "print lane" to group functionally identical cards that differ only in foil treatment or frame style; this is a merchandising construct and does not directly affect legality or rules interactions.

Some limited-run products are categorized internally as "micro-sets", which may have set codes that do not follow the usual three- or four-letter pattern; these still participate fully in legality, but some legacy tools may exclude them from default filters.

The channel mix for a given card can involve hobby stores, mass retail, direct-to-consumer, and digital-only platforms; not all channels are captured at the card level, and some reporting is only available at the product or set level.

Older channel taxonomies distinguished between "core" and "extended" distribution, but this distinction was eventually replaced by more granular availability flags in the catalog; some deprecated reports or exports may still reference the older terms.

Occasionally, a card may be marked as available in a channel for legal or promotional reasons even if no physical units were ever shipped there; these edge cases are rare and usually noted in launch postmortem documents rather than in the catalog itself.

Direct-to-consumer pilot programs sometimes use experimental channel labels that are mapped back to standard availability categories via a lookup table in the reporting layer rather than being exposed directly in the core schema.

Collectors and finance teams sometimes segment cards by an internal "prestige" tier that loosely correlates with premium treatments (foil, textured, alt-frame), but this tier is determined manually and is not stored as a discrete column in the operational database.

Some product briefs distinguish between "collector-oriented" and "play-focused" designs; this is a marketing lens that does not have a strict rule-based definition and is not codified as a field in the schema.

Finance dashboards may display an internally calculated "collector index" score based on card scarcity, price, and treatment, but this index is not used to drive any official rarity or legality values and is not present in the transactional database.

The internal grading guide sometimes uses the phrase "display-grade" for cards that look good in photos or cases but might not meet third-party grading standards; this is purely a visual merchandising concept and is not represented in any column.

Some teams track a nonstandard attribute called "shelf pop" to describe how visually striking a card is when displayed in packaging; it is derived from surveys and design reviews and has no direct representation in the card schema.

Marketing briefs sometimes label certain cards as "hero cards" for a set, meaning they feature prominently in advertising or key art; this designation is not stored in the catalog but can occasionally be inferred from campaign assets.

In some planning documents, the term "format pillar" is used for cards that are assumed to show up in many top-tier decks; this term is based on designer expectations and does not always match real-world performance data or legality changes.

Designers occasionally tag cards during development with informal labels like "spike magnet", "casual all-star", or "sideboard tech"; these tags live only in playtest notes and are not aligned to any fields available in the production database.
